---
format: revealjs
---

## Today's Agenda {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

```{r}
# background-size="1920px 1080px"
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
```

<br>

::: {.r-fit-text}

**Developing our Basic Research Tools**

- Developing your knowledge for now, AND

- Your research base for the future

:::

<br>

::: r-stack
Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2026)
:::

::: notes
Prep for Class

1. **Review Canvas submissions: NOTE great examples of each prompt!**

2. Class plan

    - 45 minutes R&R
    - 15 minutes sharing
    - 15 minutes exploring the "future research" needs identified in each article

3. Markers x MANY

<br>

**SLIDE**: Developing your research tools

:::



## Research Tools {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

<br>

::: {.r-fit-text}

- A bibliography manager: Zotero

- Top political science journals: Google Scholar

- Argument paper basics: Farrell (2010)

- Unpacking a research paper (Krippner 2000, Hoover Green 2013)

:::

::: notes

Over the last few weeks we've been introducing and applying tools and approaches to help you do scientific research.

<br>

Doing good scientific research requires you to be:

- Well informed about the world, 

- A skilled reader of published scientific work, and

- A clear communicator of ideas and arguments

<br>

Our exercises last week and this week have been designed to get you moving forward on all three of these areas.

<br>

Specifically, your work for today asks you to practice all three of these skills in one exercise!

- You learned something about the cutting edge of our knowledge, 

- You had to read strategically to extract the needed material, and

- You had the chance to communicate those ideas to your future you and the rest of us

<br>

**SLIDE**: Today I want us to reflect on your work and then spend some time focused on this third point

:::



## Research Tools {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

1. What is the research question?
2. Why is the question important?
3. What is the proposed answer to the question?
4. What real-world cases are being analyzed?
5. What are the bottom-line conclusions in the paper? 
6. How confident is the researcher in these conclusions?
7. What future research does the author suggest should follow this paper?

::: notes

Everybody basically did the exercise, but I think you've left a TON of context off the page

- You read the article so your brain automatically fills in the gaps, BUT future you won't remember the article and other people will have even less of an idea

<br>

Everybody open up the discussion board on Canvas and let's review the submissions

- Help me find examples of responses to the individual prompts that are clear, well-explained and CLEARLY rooted in the article with citations

- *Make sure RQ's get unpacked. You have to explain the concepts in the question*

- *Encourage the class to add critical analysis to each prompt! Something was confusing, under-explained or done poorly? Include that! It's gold!*

<br>

**Questions on more clearly communicating the ideas from a research article?**

<br>

Ok, take all of this on board and now we apply it!

- Everybody take the next X minutes to revise and resubmit your assignment for today (save 30 mins at end of class: 15 to share and 15 for future research)

- Use your classmates! Share ideas and get feedback as you work. 

- You want your answers clear enough that someone who hasn't read the article gets what you've summarized and explained

- Go!

<br>

Ok, 15 minutes, pair off to get and give feedback!

<br>

**SLIDE**: Our plan for the semester

:::



## {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

:::: {.r-fit-text}

**Doing good scientific research requires you to be:**

- Well informed about the world, 

- A skilled reader of published scientific work, and

- A clear communicator of ideas and arguments

::::

::: notes

Remember, all of these skills get stronger if you simply read widely

- Get in the habit of reading published scientific research and you will make progress on all three!

:::






## Course Outline {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

![](Images/05_2-Course_Outline.png){style="display: block; margin: 0 auto"}

::: notes

Today we are wrapping up the first section of our class

- Big picture aim was to help you think about the world like a political scientist

- This meant developing a healthy news diet, and rooting your knowledge in the scientific method

<br>

For the remainder of the semester we move from talking about doing research to actually doing research

- That means each of you will be designing a research proposal from scratch!

<br>

Hopefully the work we've done so far this semester has pushed you to think about a lot of different puzzles in the world you might want to explore

- **SLIDE**: Let's use the research articles you submitted today to see what research might be needed next

:::



## {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

:::: {.r-fit-text}

**Unpacking a Research Article: Future Needs**

- Go to the concluding section of the paper,

- Find the "future research" ideas, and

- Write the "BEST" ONE on the board!

::::

::: notes

In basically every piece of published research the concluding section will include some ideas the researchers have for future work

- This may be them acknowledging the limits in their current work that need to be re-examined, OR

- Entirely new research questions that follow from their work

<br>

Everybody dig into the conclusion in their paper and find the future needs ideas, OR

- You could also choose to give us something this article inspired you to think about

- I want gaps in our knowledge or questions you want answered!

- Write them on the board!

<br>

*PRESENT and DISCUSS each*

- Take notes, these ideas are gold!

<br>

**SLIDE**: For next class

:::



## For Next Class {background-image="Images/Background-Rally_v2.png" .center}

<br>

::: {.r-fit-text}

**Research Question Basics**

- Do the readings,

- Submit your reflections on Canvas

:::

::: notes

**Questions on the assignment or anything from today?**

<br>

Canvas ASSIGNMENT: Research Question Basics

Complete the two assigned readings and provide answers to the following questions. Your answers MUST cite both readings (with page numbers): 

1. Why do we need research questions? 
2. What are the characteristics of a "good" research question? 
3. What are THREE strategies we could use to generate a research question?

:::
